epic.name: Alert Routing Rules
epic.objective: Implement configurable routing rules that direct alerts to specific Slack channels, teams, or incident commanders based on alert labels, severity, and source metadata
epic.definition_of_done: Alerts are automatically routed to the correct Slack channel and/or assigned to the correct team based on matching routing rules. Rules are configurable via API and UI. Unmatched alerts use default behavior.
epic.notes: Routing rules determine WHERE an incident is handled (which channel, which team). This is distinct from grouping rules (which alerts belong together). Routing runs after grouping in the pipeline: Normalize → Group → Route → Create Incident.

tasks.name: Design routing rules data model
tasks.description: Create the data model for routing rules that determine where alerts are directed based on configurable match criteria
acceptance_criteria: RoutingRule model defined: id, name, description, enabled, priority, match_criteria (JSONB), actions (JSONB), created_at, updated_at
match_criteria supports: source (exact), severity (list), labels (exact/regex key-value pairs)
match_criteria supports AND/OR boolean logic for complex rules
actions supports: set_slack_channel (override default channel naming), set_severity (override alert severity), add_labels (enrich incident)
Actions support: notify_channel (post to an existing channel without creating new one)
Database migration: 000008_create_routing_rules_table
RoutingRuleRepository interface with CRUD + GetActiveRulesOrderedByPriority()
Seed default routing rule: critical alerts → auto-create incident (existing behavior)
tasks.implementation_notes: Routing rules are evaluated in priority order; first match wins. Default 'catch-all' rule preserves existing behavior. Actions are additive — they enrich incident properties, not replace them.

tasks.name: Implement routing rules evaluation engine
tasks.description: Build the routing engine that evaluates alerts against routing rules and returns the set of actions to apply during incident creation
acceptance_criteria: RoutingEngine service with Evaluate(alert NormalizedAlert) (*RoutingDecision, error)
RoutingDecision contains: matched_rule_id, channel_override, severity_override, extra_labels, should_create_incident, notify_channels
Evaluation: iterate rules in priority order, evaluate match_criteria, return first match
Label matching supports: exact ('env=production'), regex ('service=api-.*'), exists ('has:team'), not-exists ('!has:silenced')
Severity matching supports: list ('critical,high') or single ('critical')
Source matching supports: exact ('prometheus') or list ('prometheus,grafana')
Unmatched alerts: return default RoutingDecision (create incident if severity warrants)
Performance: evaluation completes in <5ms for 50 rules
Rules cached in memory with invalidation on CRUD operations
tasks.implementation_notes: The routing engine mirrors the pattern of the grouping engine but focuses on WHERE rather than HOW. Keep match criteria evaluation fast — use compiled regexes cached per rule.

tasks.name: Integrate routing engine into alert processing pipeline
tasks.description: Wire the routing engine between grouping and incident creation so that routing decisions affect how incidents are created
acceptance_criteria: Alert pipeline order: Normalize → Deduplicate → Group → Route → Create Incident
Routing decision applied during incident creation: channel naming, severity override, extra labels
channel_override: if set, incident Slack channel uses this name instead of default inc-{number}-{slug}
severity_override: if set, incident severity uses routing rule's severity instead of alert's
notify_channels: posts alert notification to specified channels (in addition to incident channel)
should_create_incident=false: alert stored but no incident created (suppress noisy alerts)
Timeline entry: type=routing_applied, content includes rule_name and actions taken
Backwards compatible: zero routing rules → existing behavior unchanged
tasks.implementation_notes: The routing integration point is in ProcessNormalizedAlerts() after grouping evaluation. should_create_incident=false is powerful for silencing known-noisy alerts without discarding the data.

tasks.name: Routing rules CRUD API
tasks.description: Create REST API endpoints for managing alert routing rules
acceptance_criteria: GET /api/v1/routing-rules → list all routing rules ordered by priority
GET /api/v1/routing-rules/:id → get single rule with match criteria detail
POST /api/v1/routing-rules → create rule with validation
PATCH /api/v1/routing-rules/:id → update rule
DELETE /api/v1/routing-rules/:id → soft delete rule
POST /api/v1/routing-rules/test → test a rule against a sample alert payload (dry-run)
Validation: name required, match_criteria valid schema, actions valid schema
Priority uniqueness enforced (no two rules with same priority)
Cache invalidation on mutations
tasks.implementation_notes: The /test endpoint is particularly valuable — users can verify their rules work before enabling them. Accepts a sample alert payload and returns the routing decision without actually processing it.

tasks.name: Routing rules management UI
tasks.description: Build the frontend for managing routing rules with a visual rule builder
acceptance_criteria: Settings > Routing Rules page: list all rules with drag-to-reorder priority
Each rule shows: name, match criteria summary, actions summary, enabled toggle
Create/edit rule form: name, match criteria builder (add conditions), actions builder
Match criteria builder: visual dropdowns for field (source, severity, label), operator (equals, regex, exists), value
Actions builder: checkboxes/dropdowns for channel override, severity override, suppress incident
Test rule button: input sample alert JSON, see routing decision result
Delete rule with confirmation dialog
Toast notifications for CRUD operations
tasks.implementation_notes: The visual rule builder is the most complex UI in v0.3. Start with a simple form and iterate. Drag-to-reorder can use the same DnD library as the incident board (if present) or react-beautiful-dnd.

tasks.name: Integration tests for routing rules
tasks.description: Comprehensive tests for routing engine, API, and pipeline integration
acceptance_criteria: Test: alert matching routing rule → channel override applied to incident
Test: alert matching suppress rule → alert stored, no incident created
Test: alert matching severity override → incident created with overridden severity
Test: alert matching no rule → default behavior (existing incident creation logic)
Test: multiple rules → first match (highest priority) wins
Test: routing rules CRUD API endpoints
Test: /test endpoint returns correct dry-run result
Test: label regex matching (valid and invalid patterns)
Test: rule disable → alert not matched by disabled rule
All tests pass without external dependencies
tasks.implementation_notes: Include edge cases: empty match_criteria (matches all), invalid regex (returns error on create), concurrent rule evaluation.

internal: Alert grouping engine (OI-EPIC-013, optional — routing works without grouping)
